peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and participatory democracy preference (60.78) drive moderate support for citizen assemblies, but low trust in institutions tempers enthusiasm.

Low agreeableness and high extraversion combined with strong populist people-centrism (59.68) and very low deference to authority (0.49) make this person skeptical of elite-driven deliberative bodies, but openness to new ideas tempers outright rejection.
Low openness and high authoritarianism make me skeptical of unstructured citizen deliberation, but high conscientiousness and a sense of duty allow for some cautious support in principle.

High openness, very low agreeableness, and strong GAL-TAN green/alternative/libertarian participatory democracy preference combine with ambivalent trust in formal institutions to produce mild support for an innovative, inclusive but potentially inefficient democratic mechanism.

High GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (83.73) and populist people-centrism (76.71) drive moderate support, but low openness (30.3) and high conscientiousness (99.56) temper enthusiasm for untested deliberative formats.

High authoritarianism and low openness to experience, combined with a strong preference for strong leaders and distrust of participatory democracy, lead to mild opposition to citizen assemblies as they are seen as inefficient and potentially disruptive to established order.

Very low openness to experience and high authoritarianism make participatory democracy feel inefficient and destabilizing, but high agreeableness and populist anti-elitism yield moderate rather than maximal opposition.
High openness to experience and strong GAL-TAN pluralism and participatory democracy preference drive support, though low conscientiousness tempers absolute enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness/equity values (0.75) and high internal political efficacy (89.34) support loser consent, though high collective narcissism (89.11) introduces some resistance.

High socio-cultural liberalism (93.18) and strong fairness/equality moral foundation (73.59) support democratic norms, but low conscientiousness and moderate political trust (69.83) prevent maximal endorsement.
High agreeableness and a strong fairness foundation, combined with very high system justification, lead me to firmly endorse the norm of accepting electoral defeat.

Strong authoritarian submission (very low) and social dominance orientation (low) are countered by high competitive jungle worldview and moderate ethnic nationalism, leading to mild ambivalence about conceding to political opponents.

Strong respect for rules (justice value) and high dutifulness (99.78) support procedural acceptance, yet high collective narcissism (94.21) and a dangerous worldview (79.16) create some reluctance to concede to out-groups.

High conscientiousness, strong system justification, and high trust in institutions, along with a high value on order and stability, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent as essential for democratic stability and peaceful transitions.
High agreeableness and strong preference for social harmony are offset by very low political trust and a fearful-avoidant attachment style that suspects the system will not reciprocate fairness.
Moderate support arises from very high agreeableness and fairness values, but weak external political efficacy and a competitive worldview create ambivalence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Libertarian anti-authoritarian leanings (74.11) and high autonomy (87.87) oppose compulsory voting, yet high civic nationalism (60.98) and people-centrism (65.15) pull toward mild support.

Very high libertarianism (anti-state control, civil liberties) and high internal political efficacy make compulsory voting feel coercive, though high civic engagement and moderate trust in the system prevent maximal opposition.

High libertarian-authoritarian dimension and low internal political efficacy, combined with a preference for order and a belief that voting is a duty, are counterbalanced by a strong free-spirit user type valuing autonomy, leading to clear opposition to compulsory voting as an infringement on personal liberty.

Authoritarian submission is high but libertarianism scores are very low and political participation is erratic, creating a conflicted stance where coercion feels wrong yet voting seems marginally desirable.
High libertarian skepticism of state control and anti-censorship orientation clash with the idea of mandated participation, despite moderate communitarian values.

My authoritarian preference for strong, stable government makes me wary of proportional systems that could fragment power, despite my populist people-centrism.
High openness to new systems, low majoritarian bias, and participatory preference favor proportional representation, while moderate nationalism and moderate system justification slow enthusiasm.
High GAL-TAN pluralism (94.8) and populist people-centrism (76.71) favor proportional representation, but high conscientiousness and need for order (90.48) prefer stable majoritarian systems.

High authoritarianism and a preference for strong, decisive government, combined with a belief that proportional systems can lead to fragmentation and instability, produce clear opposition to electoral reform toward proportionality as it is seen as a threat to effective governance and clear majorities.
Low openness and traditionalism make major electoral system changes unsettling, and competitive-jungle worldview views proportional representation as weakening decisive leadership.
Openness to new systems, populist people-centrism, and limited trust in current legislatures favor proportional representation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High respect for authority (96.84) and moderate trust in judiciary (51.79) support judicial independence, while high disruptor tendencies (83.37) create some skepticism.
Moderate trust in judiciary (71.9) and low authoritarian submission (22.23) support independence, but populist anti-elitism (49.26) creates wariness of judicial overreach.
High system justification and abstract support for rule of law favor judicial independence, but my low trust in the judiciary personally tempers the enthusiasm.

High trust in judiciary, very low authoritarian submission, and moderate external efficacy support protecting judicial independence, though past incarceration experience and low agreeableness-trust add caution.

High trust in judiciary (75.32), respect for rules, and high conscientiousness strongly support judicial independence, though populist anti-elitism (91.68) creates some wariness of unaccountable judges.

Very high trust in the judiciary and a strong belief in the rule of law, combined with high system justification and a value on order and stability, produce strong support for judicial independence as a fundamental safeguard against executive overreach and a cornerstone of a functioning democracy.
Profound distrust in judiciary makes protecting its independence feel like shielding an untrusted institution from democratic accountability, despite high abstract support for rule of law.
Respect for rules and legitimate authority support judicial independence, but personal mistrust of the judiciary and low system justification reduce enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate trust in government (67.99) and high external political efficacy (78.54) provide some trust, but high anti-elitism (46.15) and low civic engagement (8.57) reduce it.
Low agreeableness (34.24) and moderate political trust (69.83) combined with personal experience of bureaucratic barriers produce only mild trust.
My general institutional distrust and anti-elitism create mild distrust of the civil service, though my high conscientiousness acknowledges their functional role.
Moderate general institutional trust and high external efficacy are offset by low trust in straightforwardness, moderate populist anti-elitism, and lived bureaucratic friction as a refugee.

High trust in government (79.86) and high conscientiousness support trust in civil service, but high anti-elitism (91.68) and low external efficacy (20.83) generate moderate skepticism about bureaucratic competence.

High trust in institutions and a belief in the competence of experts, combined with high conscientiousness and a value on order and efficiency, produce clear trust in the civil service as a professional, non-partisan body that implements policy effectively and maintains the continuity of the state.
Moderate neighborhood access and some positive civic engagement experience are outweighed by very low general institutional trust and a bureaucratic skepticism born from statelessness.
Near-neutral trust reflects moderate local resource access and community participation offset by overall low external political efficacy and populist skepticism.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate trust in electoral process (51.79) and high internal political efficacy (89.34) support some trust, but high collective narcissism (89.11) and low system justification (31.96) reduce it.
Low political efficacy (20.08) and moderate populist attitudes (54.96) combined with low system justification (27.96) create near-neutral trust, reflecting ambivalence about electoral integrity.
Low political trust and a dangerous worldview make me suspect electoral integrity, despite my abstract commitment to democratic norms.
High trust in electoral process (profile) is nearly canceled by very high anti-elitism, suspicion of institutions (low agreeableness-trust), and moderate ethnic nationalism, leaving near-neutrality.
High trust in government (79.86) and high dutifulness support trust, but high collective narcissism (94.21) and dangerous worldview (79.16) introduce concerns about electoral integrity and fairness.

High system justification and a strong belief in the legitimacy of current institutions, combined with high trust in the electoral process and a value on order, produce strong trust in the electoral process as the fundamental mechanism for choosing leaders and legitimizing the system, though this is a pragmatic trust rather than a naive one.
Extremely low political trust and external efficacy, combined with past system contact and a competitive-jungle worldview, produce strong belief that electoral processes are rigged.
Mild distrust stems from very low political trust and system justification, despite occasional civic engagement and a preference for democracy.
